questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a 15-year history of heartburn presents for upper GI endoscopy. He has not had previous endoscopy. Endoscopy shows a Z-line at 38 cm from incisors with salmon-pink mucosa extending 4 cm proximally. Biopsies from this area show intestinal metaplasia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of salmon-pink mucosa extending above the gastro-oesophageal junction with intestinal metaplasia on biopsy is diagnostic of Barrett's oesophagus. Reflux oesophagitis shows linear erosions, not salmon-pink mucosa. Adenocarcinoma would typically present as a mass.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive dysphagia to solids and weight loss. He has a history of heartburn for many years. Upper GI endoscopy shows a smooth, benign-appearing stricture at 32 cm from incisors, with salmon-pink mucosa proximal to the stricture. Biopsies from the salmon-pink area show intestinal metaplasia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of a benign stricture with adjacent salmon-pink mucosa showing intestinal metaplasia confirms Barrett's oesophagus. Adenocarcinoma typically presents as an irregular, ulcerated mass. Peptic stricture without Barrett's would not have columnar-lined oesophagus.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with iron deficiency anaemia (Hb 90 g/L, ferritin 8 μg/L). She has no dyspepsia or heartburn. Upper GI endoscopy shows a segment of columnar-lined oesophagus extending 3 cm above the gastro-oesophageal junction, with a single shallow erosion in the columnar area. Biopsies confirm intestinal metaplasia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anaemia can be caused by occult bleeding from Barrett's-associated oesophagitis or ulcer. The endoscopic finding of columnar-lined oesophagus with intestinal metaplasia confirms Barrett's. Adenocarcinoma is less likely given the absence of a mass.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man undergoes upper GI endoscopy for investigation of dyspepsia. He denies heartburn or regurgitation. Endoscopy reveals an irregular Z-line with tongues of salmon-pink mucosa extending 2 cm above the gastro-oesophageal junction. Biopsies from this area show intestinal metaplasia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Intestinal metaplasia in columnar epithelium above the GEJ is diagnostic of Barrett's oesophagus, even in the absence of reflux symptoms. Hiatal hernia does not contain intestinal metaplasia. Gastric metaplasia at the GEJ is common but does not extend like tongues.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a known history of Barrett's oesophagus presents with haematemesis and melaena. Urgent endoscopy reveals an actively bleeding, clean-based ulcer within a segment of Barrett's mucosa, with no other source of bleeding. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The underlying diagnosis remains Barrett's oesophagus; the ulcer is a complication of Barrett's. Adenocarcinoma would appear as a mass, not a clean-based ulcer. Mallory-Weiss tear occurs at the gastro-oesophageal junction after vomiting. Varices are seen in portal hypertension.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a 10-year history of heartburn and regurgitation undergoes upper GI endoscopy. The endoscopist notes a 3 cm tongue of salmon-coloured mucosa extending above the gastro-oesophageal junction. What is the single most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis of Barrett's oesophagus?
options: Quadrantic biopsies every 2 cm of the columnar-lined segment
CT scan of chest and abdomen
questions.explanation: Systematic biopsies following the Seattle protocol (quadrantic every 2 cm) are required to confirm intestinal metaplasia histologically, which is diagnostic of Barrett's. Other tests assess function or anatomy but cannot confirm metaplasia.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with long-standing GORD is offered the Cytosponge test as part of a screening programme. The result is positive for TFF3. What is the single most appropriate next investigation to confirm Barrett's oesophagus?
options: CT scan of chest and abdomen
Upper GI endoscopy with quadrantic biopsies
questions.explanation: Cytosponge is a screening test for Barrett's; a positive TFF3 result indicates columnar metaplasia but requires endoscopic confirmation with biopsies to demonstrate intestinal metaplasia. Other tests are not confirmatory.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known Barrett's oesophagus presents with haematemesis and melaena. After resuscitation, upper GI endoscopy reveals a 2 cm ulcerated lesion at the gastro-oesophageal junction. Biopsies are taken. What is the single most important next step in diagnosis?
options: CT scan of chest and abdomen
Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) of the lesion
questions.explanation: EMR provides a large specimen for accurate histological assessment of depth of invasion and grade of dysplasia, essential for staging early oesophageal adenocarcinoma in Barrett's. Biopsies alone may be superficial. Staging investigations are secondary to histological diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old obese woman with reflux symptoms is being evaluated for laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy. Pre-operative upper GI endoscopy shows a 1 cm tongue of salmon-coloured mucosa at the gastro-oesophageal junction. Biopsies from this area reveal intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells. What is the most appropriate diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Intestinal metaplasia in columnar-lined oesophagus, regardless of length, defines Barrett's oesophagus. This is a short-segment Barrett's (<3 cm). Other options do not include the required histological feature.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on surveillance for non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus undergoes routine endoscopy. Biopsies from the columnar segment show glandular architecture with mild atypia, but the changes are insufficient to confidently diagnose dysplasia. What is the correct histological diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The description fits 'indefinite for dysplasia', a category used when architectural or cytological changes are equivocal. Clear criteria exist for the other categories; this case lacks them.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a 10-year history of Barrett's oesophagus undergoes surveillance endoscopy. Biopsies from a flat, non-visible Barrett's segment show high-grade dysplasia. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For high-grade dysplasia in flat Barrett's, radiofrequency ablation (RFA) is first-line per NICE. Endoscopic mucosal resection is reserved for visible lesions.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with Barrett's oesophagus (C2M3, no dysplasia) on no medication undergoes surveillance. She has no reflux symptoms. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: For non-dysplastic Barrett's without symptoms, endoscopic surveillance is recommended. PPIs are offered only for symptom control, not chemoprevention.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known Barrett's oesophagus presents with dysphagia. OGD shows a tight oesophageal stricture at 30 cm with surrounding erosive oesophagitis. Biopsies are negative for dysplasia. He is already on omeprazole 40mg daily. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: Benign oesophageal stricture in Barrett's is managed with endoscopic dilatation. Increasing PPI dose may help prevent recurrence but does not treat the stricture.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old pregnant woman (12 weeks gestation) with known Barrett's oesophagus complains of severe heartburn. What is the most appropriate first-line antireflux therapy?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, antacids or alginates are first-line for mild-to-moderate symptoms. PPIs are reserved for refractory cases. NICE guideline.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with Barrett's oesophagus underwent successful radiofrequency ablation for high-grade dysplasia 6 months ago. Follow-up biopsies show no dysplasia or intestinal metaplasia. He is asymptomatic on omeprazole 20mg daily. What is the recommended long-term management?
options: Switch omeprazole to ranitidine 150mg twice daily
questions.explanation: After successful ablation, long-term acid suppression with a PPI is recommended to prevent recurrence of metaplasia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a 10-year history of heartburn undergoes oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) which shows a 3 cm segment of salmon-coloured mucosa above the gastro-oesophageal junction. Biopsies confirm intestinal metaplasia without dysplasia. He is currently taking omeprazole 20 mg daily with good symptom control. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange urgent repeat OGD within 6 weeks
Perform CT scan of chest and abdomen
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend surveillance OGD every 3–5 years for non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus. High-dose PPI is not indicated as symptoms are controlled. Endoscopic resection is for visible lesions or dysplasia. Urgent repeat OGD or CT is not required without dysplasia or symptoms.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with known non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus attends clinic with intermittent heartburn despite omeprazole 20 mg daily. She has no alarm symptoms. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Optimising PPI dose is the first step in managing persistent reflux symptoms. NICE advises increasing to a standard dose (e.g., omeprazole 40 mg) before adding other agents or considering surgery. OGD is not urgent without alarm features.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known Barrett's oesophagus presents to the Emergency Department with haematemesis and melaena. He is haemodynamically stable (HR 90, BP 110/70). What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells
Start intravenous proton pump inhibitor therapy
Perform CT angiogram of the abdomen
questions.explanation: In acute upper GI bleed with haemodynamic stability, immediate IV PPI reduces rebleeding and need for endoscopic intervention. Transfusion is reserved for haemodynamic instability or Hb <70 g/L. OGD is planned but not immediate. CT angiogram or balloon tamponade are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with Barrett's oesophagus and previous low-grade dysplasia (confirmed on two occasions) presents with progressive dysphagia and unintentional weight loss over 3 months. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange CT scan of chest and abdomen
Start a course of oral steroids
questions.explanation: New dysphagia and weight loss in Barrett's suggest progression to high-grade dysplasia or adenocarcinoma. Urgent OGD with systematic biopsies is the first investigation to confirm and stage. Barium swallow is less sensitive. CT may follow but OGD is immediate. Steroids or surgery are not first steps without diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus (diagnosed 12 months ago) attends follow-up. She is asymptomatic on omeprazole 20 mg daily. She asks about future risk and surveillance. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Advise lifestyle measures and schedule surveillance OGD in 3 years
questions.explanation: NICE recommends surveillance OGD every 3–5 years for non-dysplastic Barrett's. Lifestyle advice (weight loss, smoking cessation) should be reinforced. Discharge is not appropriate as surveillance is needed. Ablation is for dysplasia. CT is not for surveillance.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known Barrett's oesophagus presents with a 4-week history of progressive dysphagia to solids and unintentional weight loss of 6 kg. On physical examination, which of the following findings is most concerning for metastatic oesophageal adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: A palpable left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow's node) in a patient with dysphagia and weight loss suggests cervical lymph node metastasis from oesophageal cancer, indicating stage IV disease and poor prognosis.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of Barrett's oesophagus undergoes routine surveillance endoscopy. Biopsies from the Barrett's segment are taken. Which histological finding would necessitate immediate referral to a specialist centre for consideration of endoscopic therapy?
questions.explanation: High-grade dysplasia in Barrett's oesophagus carries a high risk of progression to adenocarcinoma. Current NICE guidelines recommend referral for further assessment and consideration of endoscopic resection or ablation.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with long-standing Barrett's oesophagus presents to the Emergency Department with sudden severe retrosternal chest pain and haematemesis. He is hypotensive (BP 85/50 mmHg) and tachycardic (HR 120 bpm). A chest X-ray is performed. Which radiographic finding would confirm the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of violent retching (or in this case, haematemesis) with chest pain and pneumomediastinum is classic for oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave syndrome). This is a surgical emergency requiring immediate drainage and repair.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known Barrett's oesophagus presents with a 6-week history of progressive hoarseness. She has no cough or sore throat. Laryngoscopy reveals a left vocal cord palsy. Which of the following is the most likely cause?
options: Oesophageal carcinoma invading the left recurrent laryngeal nerve
questions.explanation: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy in a patient with Barrett's oesophagus is highly suggestive of invasion by oesophageal adenocarcinoma. This is a red flag for advanced disease and requires urgent staging CT.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with Barrett's oesophagus is found to have iron deficiency anaemia (Hb 90 g/L, MCV 70 fL) on routine blood tests. He has no dysphagia or weight loss. Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy shows a 2 cm raised, irregular ulcer at the gastro-oesophageal junction. Biopsies confirm intramucosal adenocarcinoma. Which of the following findings on endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) would be most concerning for lymph node metastasis?
options: A perioesophageal lymph node >1 cm in short axis
questions.explanation: In oesophageal cancer, lymph node size >1 cm with round shape and hypoechoic texture on EUS is highly suspicious for metastasis. For T1a cancers, lymph node metastasis is rare, but T1b carries a risk. However, the most direct finding of nodal involvement is an enlarged lymph node. Note: T stage itself does not confirm lymph node involvement.
